message.content:
Adversarially VERIFY this research claim about how a top deep-research system scores on DeepResearch Bench.

CLAIM: Under the GPT-5.5 judge, READABILITY is the dimension where even SOTA barely beats the human reference, while INSIGHT and COMPREHENSIVENESS are where the headroom is. The Gemini judge inflated insight; GPT-5.5 compresses it.
DETAIL: GPT-5.5 tab, top-6 systems: readability spans 49.68-53.20 (mean ~51.6) while insight spans 52.22-57.08 and comprehensiveness 50.53-56.34. Same systems on the Gemini tab show insight far higher relative to readability (cellcog-max: insight 60.01 vs readability 53.21 under Gemini; insight 57.08 vs readability 51.94 under GPT-5.5). So under GPT-5.5 the judge is stingier on insight and roughly flat on readability — nobody scores >53.2 readability. Our 37.74 readability is ~14 pts below the SOTA band and ~12 below the human reference: that is the single largest per-dimension deficit we have, even though its weight is only 0.14 on task 72.
SYSTEM: other
SOURCE GIVEN: https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/raw/main/data_gpt55/leaderboard.csv
STATED CONFIDENCE: high

Go to the source. Check it actually says this. Check it is CURRENT (the benchmark's leaderboard and evaluator both
changed in 2026 — the original paper's numbers are stale; Gemini-2.5-Pro was replaced by GPT-5.5 as the RACE judge).
Default to UNSUPPORTED if you cannot confirm it from a primary source. We would rather have five verified facts
than thirty plausible ones.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
Based on the provided content, I cannot answer your questions because the code snippet shown is minimal and incomplete.

The file is merely an entry point that imports and launches a demo application. It contains no information about:

- Judge/tab definitions
- Data directories or their configurations
- Default or current directory settings
- Text comparing Gemini-2.5-Pro versus GPT-5.5 models
- Reference report baselines or RACE judge model details

To find these specifications, you would need to examine the `create_leaderboard` module and the `demo` object's configuration, which are not included in this excerpt.

sota_review/codex_plan.md:248:**Why it should move score:** These defects visibly depress readability and make the review look mechanically corrupted. Removing them also reduces distraction and off-topic material.
sota_review/codex_plan.md:252:**Measurement:** Require zero artifact occurrences as a release gate, but treat that only as verification. The success measure is a repeated evaluator increase in the named readability criteria and instruction focus—not the number of strings removed.
DRIVER_BRIEF_V2.md:33:- **The readability defects we blamed for months are GHOSTS** — the `(also mirrored)` markers and tier labels are
race_task72_criteria.txt:4:DIMENSION WEIGHTS: {"readability": 0.14, "insight": 0.32, "comprehensiveness": 0.29, "instruction_following": 0.25}
race_task72_criteria.txt:36:- [w=0.10] F1: Formatting, Layout, and Visual Consistency :: Evaluates the use of consistent and professional formatting (e.g., font choice and size, spacing, margins, heading styles) that enhances readability. Assesses the overall visual layout for ease of navigation and reduced reader fatigue, including clear paragraph breaks. Also considers the visual consistency and unobtrusive integration of in-text citations and the clarity of the bibliography's formatting, which contributes to the academic presentation.
FLYWHEEL_PROGRESS.md:100:  - RACE (Fable, 1-5): comprehensiveness **2.5 -> 4**, insight **2.5 -> 3**, instruction-following **3 -> 3**, readability **2.5 -> 2**.
FLYWHEEL_PROGRESS.md:127:  | arm | words | OVERALL | comprehens. | insight | instr-follow | readability |
FLYWHEEL_PROGRESS.md:139:  - **THE READABILITY DEFECTS WE BLAMED ARE GHOSTS.** The 25 `(also mirrored)` markers and 105 tier labels — Fable's headline readability finding, repeated all night — are STRIPPED by the cleaner and NEVER touched our score. Hygiene = 0 points. (What DOES survive and is judge-visible: the telemetry confession *"No contradictions were detected by the pipeline"*, sitting inside the section graded for critical synthesis.)
FLYWHEEL_PROGRESS.md:149:- 2026-07-13 03:20 | **RANK12 (SOURCE-ELIGIBILITY FIREWALL) SCORED: 0.4276 — SCORE-NEUTRAL. THE DRIVER'S "CLEANEST WIN OF THE NIGHT" WAS WORTH NOTHING.** comprehens 0.4562 | insight 0.4133 | instr-follow 0.4280 | readability 0.3979. It sits INSIDE the Rank9/10/11 noise band (0.4133-0.4313). **The tell: the firewall was aimed at INSTRUCTION-FOLLOWING (T1-T3 bibliography compliance 43.3% -> 72.8%, +29.5 pts) and instruction-following on the SCORECARD went 0.4409 (Rank10) -> 0.4280 — DOWN, within noise. The lever aimed at the dimension did not move the dimension.** Exactly as the ArticleCleaner finding predicts: the judge never sees our citations, so citation quality cannot score. **THIRD independent confirmation, at zero extra cost: length -> no movement; padding removal -> no movement; source compliance -> no movement. EVERY lever pulled since Rank7 has been INVISIBLE TO THE SCORER.**

=== FW tail ===
FLYWHEEL_PROGRESS.md:100:  - RACE (Fable, 1-5): comprehensiveness **2.5 -> 4**, insight **2.5 -> 3**, instruction-following **3 -> 3**, readability **2.5 -> 2**.
FLYWHEEL_PROGRESS.md:127:  | arm | words | OVERALL | comprehens. | insight | instr-follow | readability |
FLYWHEEL_PROGRESS.md:139:  - **THE READABILITY DEFECTS WE BLAMED ARE GHOSTS.** The 25 `(also mirrored)` markers and 105 tier labels — Fable's headline readability finding, repeated all night — are STRIPPED by the cleaner and NEVER touched our score. Hygiene = 0 points. (What DOES survive and is judge-visible: the telemetry confession *"No contradictions were detected by the pipeline"*, sitting inside the section graded for critical synthesis.)
- 2026-07-13 02:15 | **FABLE GATE #3 — THE 4th ARTIFACT, AND IT WAS MY OWN INSTRUMENT FLATTERING THE ARM I WAS SELLING.** Fable re-keyed my density metric through the corpus rows and got **68 works @ 8.78/1k** where I reported **94 @ 12.14**. I verified it: **`flywheel_density.py` read `title`, but the bibliography key is `source_title`** — so every title normalized to `""`, the fallback keyed each entry by its own ev_id, and **NOT ONE MIRROR WAS EVER COLLAPSED.** Worse, when I fixed that, a *second* ordering bug surfaced: **DOI-before-title means a work WITH a doi never meets its doi-LESS mirror** — `[1]` Acemoglu-Restrepo (T1, AEA JEP, DOI) and `[13]`, the IZA `[PDF]` scrape of the *same paper* (T4, no DOI), counted as **two works**. Same for "Generative AI at Work" `[42]` vs its arXiv mirror `[78]`. **No single key identifies a work — the mirrors disagree about which field they carry.** Rebuilt on **UNION-FIND** (merge on same_work_group OR doi OR title-prefix). Honest numbers: CTRL **36 works @ 14.47/1k**, RANK10 **86 @ 11.11/1k**. Still less aggressive than Fable's 68, but **the direction and every conclusion are identical.** *The error inflated the LONG arms specifically — they cite more mirrors. An instrument whose bias points at your own hypothesis is the most dangerous kind, and this is the FOURTH time tonight a measurement of mine returned a plausible number through a broken path.*
- 2026-07-13 02:15 | **THE FINDING THAT MATTERS MOST TONIGHT — AND IT IS THE OPERATOR'S IDEA, WHICH I KILLED AT 21:55: TIER COMPLIANCE COLLAPSED AS THE REPORT GREW.** Cited-entry T1-T3 share: **CTRL 57.8% -> RANK7 53.7% -> RANK9 45.4% -> RANK10 43.3%.** DRB task 72 instructs *"only cite high-quality, English-language journal articles"* — so **the depth push made INSTRUCTION-FOLLOWING WORSE**, and this metric is **deterministic (no noise)**. Rank10 cites a **Toptal freelancer listicle, an amplifai blog, apporya.com, a Substack, hrexecutive.com**, while **112 T1-T3 works that survived the judge are never cited at all.**
- 2026-07-13 02:15 | **I WAS WRONG TO RETIRE EXPERIMENT #1, AND I WAS WRONG IN A SPECIFIC, NAMEABLE WAY.** At 21:55 I killed the SOURCE-ELIGIBILITY FIREWALL because *"tier is not relevance"* — 56 T1/T2 rows sit in the junk bucket, so a tier prior deletes nothing that matters. **That reasoning is CORRECT for the DELETION axis and IRRELEVANT to the axis the brief was actually about.** The firewall's job is **CITATION ELIGIBILITY** — what the writer is ALLOWED TO CITE — and that is an **explicit task-72 instruction**, not a heuristic. **Two different uses of "tier"; I conflated them and threw out the right one.** The operator's experiment #1 was right; my refutation answered a question nobody asked.
- 2026-07-13 02:15 | **ROOT CAUSE (verified in source): `_apply_writer_menu_cap` takes `ev_subset[:cap]` — the HEAD of a list ranked by RELEVANCE ONLY** (multi_section_generator.py:5997). Nothing in that path has ever seen `tier`. So the 30 writer slots fill with whatever the router ranked top, and **a T4 `[PDF]` scrape outranks the T1 journal version of the SAME WORK.** The cap I have been praising all night as "free depth" was **simultaneously destroying tier compliance** — it made the menu smaller without making it better.
- 2026-07-13 02:15 | **RANK12 LANDED IN CODE + LAUNCHED: `_tier_first_menu()` (`PG_WRITER_MENU_TIER_FIRST`, default-OFF).** STABLE-sorted, applied **BEFORE** the cap takes its head-N (reordering *after* truncation would only shuffle rows already chosen — a silent no-op, the exact failure class that has bitten me three times tonight). **Stable** so rows keep relevance order WITHIN a tier: this raises quality **among equals**, it does **not** re-rank on quality INSTEAD of relevance — an off-topic T1 row is still off-topic, and the **topic judge remains the instrument for that**. WRITER PROMPT ONLY: `ev_ids`, `evidence_pool`, bibliography and the frozen faithfulness engine untouched; every sentence still gated against the FULL pool. Unknown/missing tiers sort **LAST**, never raise. **11 tests**, incl. the one that counts: cap alone fills the 2 slots with **T4/T7** (defect reproduced); tier-first + cap fills them with **T1/T2** (fix). Running now alongside Rank11 (repetition guard — Fable predicts it catches **0-2**, since it is EXACT-match and every duplicate found was a PARAPHRASE).

- 2026-07-13 03:00 | **RANK12 — THE SCORE ARRIVED, AND IT REFUTES THE HEADLINE OF THIS ENTIRE LOG.** Every depth arm was scored with the OFFICIAL RACE harness (task 72, gpt-5.5 evaluator = the CURRENT leaderboard evaluator). Until tonight NOT ONE arm had ever been scored; we optimised proxies (words, verified sentences, distinct works, density) for a full night and never once checked the instrument that grades us.
  | arm | words | OVERALL | comprehens. | insight | instr-follow | readability |
  |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
  | CTRL baseline | 3,343 | 0.4052 | 0.4310 | 0.3839 | 0.4032 | 0.4008 |
  | Rank7 | 7,615 | 0.4041 | 0.4326 | 0.4070 | 0.3996 | 0.3379 |
  | Rank8 | 4,226 | 0.3957 | 0.4197 | 0.3903 | 0.3907 | 0.3634 |
  | Rank9 | 7,937 | 0.4155 | 0.4372 | 0.4122 | 0.4241 | 0.3588 |
  | Rank10 | 9,194 | 0.4313 | 0.4549 | 0.4238 | 0.4409 | 0.3774 |
  | Rank11 (repetition guard) | 7,178 | 0.4133 | 0.4391 | 0.3973 | 0.4212 | 0.3802 |
  **Rank9/10 are the SAME config => their 0.0158 gap IS the noise floor. Rank11 sits INSIDE it. Ranks 9/10/11 mean ~0.42 and are statistically indistinguishable.** Baseline 0.4052 -> best ~0.42 = **+0.015, about ONE noise-width, for the whole night's 2.7x depth push.** Quoting Rank10's 0.4313 as "our score" is cherry-picking the luckiest draw. **The 2.7x words / 2.7x verified sentences / 2.2x distinct works headline DID NOT BUY SCORE.**
- 2026-07-13 03:00 | **RANK11 (cross-section repetition guard, the 3rd dark flag): SCORE-NEUTRAL.** It deleted ~2,000 words (9,194 -> 7,178) and the score did not move outside noise. The padding was real and it was worth nothing — but removing it was also worth nothing. Clean cheap negative.
- 2026-07-13 03:00 | **THE DECISIVE MECHANISM FINDING — RACE STRIPS THE BIBLIOGRAPHY AND EVERY CITATION MARKER BEFORE THE JUDGE READS A WORD.** `deepresearch_bench_race.py` runs `utils/clean_article.ArticleCleaner` (an LLM pass: "remove all citation links, citation marks, reference lists, footnotes"). VERIFIED by diffing our submitted vs judge-read artifact: **9,300 words -> 7,692; [n] markers 345 -> 0; `## References` (105 entries) -> DELETED; `(also mirrored)` 25 -> 0; `(tier T6)` labels 105 -> 0.** CONSEQUENCES:
  - **ALL BIBLIOGRAPHY-SIDE WORK SCORES ZERO.** The source-eligibility firewall (DOI/journal/tier metadata, mirror dedup, purging Toptal/blog entries) is INVISIBLE to the judge. The instruction-following criterion "Exclusive Citation of High-Quality Journal Articles" (w=0.15) can ONLY be reached via **venue names written in the running prose**. (The 0.5000 reference ships ZERO inline citations and no bibliography at all.)
  - **THE READABILITY DEFECTS WE BLAMED ARE GHOSTS.** The 25 `(also mirrored)` markers and 105 tier labels — Fable's headline readability finding, repeated all night — are STRIPPED by the cleaner and NEVER touched our score. Hygiene = 0 points. (What DOES survive and is judge-visible: the telemetry confession *"No contradictions were detected by the pipeline"*, sitting inside the section graded for critical synthesis.)
- 2026-07-13 03:00 | **THE REAL GAP, measured on the text the judge ACTUALLY READS (post-cleaner):** ours **12 body paragraphs averaging 633 words, 0 H3, 0 tables, 0 bullets**; reference **59 paragraphs averaging 142 words, 24 H3, 10 tables, 91 bullets**. **Our report is twelve walls of text; the reference is a navigable argument.** Two independent reviewers (Codex 5.6 max-thinking + Fable, each reading both full reports line-by-line, blind to each other) converged: *the reference converts evidence into an ARGUMENT; POLARIS converts retrieved passages into SENTENCES.* **This is architectural: `strict_verify` requires every sentence to be span-grounded, which STRUCTURALLY FORBIDS the interpretive sentence ("this discrepancy might reflect implementation lags...") that the reference uses to earn insight (0.32, the heaviest dimension and our worst at ~0.42).** We did not fail to write synthesis — we built a machine that cannot write it.

input.prompt: How is the RACE score computed relative to the reference report? Does a report that ties the reference score 50? What judge model is used, and has it changed to GPT-5.5? Quote exactly.

content.content:
# RACE Scoring and Judge Model

**How RACE scores are computed:**
The document describes RACE as using "Dynamic Criteria Generation" that "Automatically generates task-specific evaluation criteria" and "Reference-Based Scoring" that "Compares target reports against high-quality reference reports." However, the specific mathematical formula for computing scores relative to the reference report is not detailed.

**Tie scoring:**
The document does not specify whether a report matching the reference score would receive 50 points or any other particular value.

**Judge model status:**
Yes, the evaluator has changed to GPT-5.5. The document states: "Following Google's announced June 17, 2026 deprecation of Gemini-2.5-Pro, we benchmarked three frontier reasoning models...GPT-5.5 wins on Overall, PAR, and FAS. We are adopting it as the new RACE evaluator (with GPT-5.4-mini for the FACT pipeline)."

content.text: Let me confirm the RACE formula (does tying the reference score 50?).

input.prompt: Show the exact formula used to convert target vs reference criterion scores into the final RACE score. Does a target that ties the reference get 50?

input.description: Check local notes on reference baseline

content.content:
/home/polaris/polaris_project/FLYWHEEL_PROGRESS.md:138:  - **ALL BIBLIOGRAPHY-SIDE WORK SCORES ZERO.** The source-eligibility firewall (DOI/journal/tier metadata, mirror dedup, purging Toptal/blog entries) is INVISIBLE to the judge. The instruction-following criterion "Exclusive Citation of High-Quality Journal Articles" (w=0.15) can ONLY be reached via **venue names written in the running prose**. (The 0.5000 reference ships ZERO inline citations and no bibliography at all.)
/home/polaris/polaris_project/FLYWHEEL_PROGRESS.md:141:- 2026-07-13 03:00 | **TARGET CORRECTED: SOTA = 0.5265 (ADORE), not 0.4888.** The 48.88 Gemini-2.5-Pro number in benchmark_target.md is the ORIGINAL PAPER's and is STALE. Current leaderboard: ADORE 0.5265, Tavily 0.5244 — **both have BEATEN the human reference (>0.50)**. Gap from our ~0.42 is **~10 points, not ~6**. Caveat: leaderboard = 100-task mean; ours = task 72, n=1.

# RACE Score Formula

The code shows the formula for converting target vs reference scores into the final RACE score:

**Overall Score Formula:**
```
overall_score = target_total / (target_total + reference_total)
```

**Yes, a tie yields 50.** When target and reference scores are equal, the formula produces exactly 0.5 (50%).

The same normalization applies to individual dimensions:
```
normalized_dim = target_score / (target_score + reference_score)
```

This is a comparative scoring approach where: target scoring 100% against an equal-scoring reference yields 0.5, target scoring higher yields >0.5, and lower yields <0.5. The code calculates "target / (target + reference)" for the "overall_score" and each dimension ("comprehensiveness," "insight," "instruction_following," "readability").

content.text: All numbers checked against the live primary source. Here is my verification.

input.reasoning:
I fetched the cited primary source live (https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/raw/main/data_gpt55/leaderboard.csv). It returns exactly 10 rows and every number in the claim reproduces verbatim.

CURRENCY CHECK (passed). The official benchmark repo README (https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench) states: "Following Google's announced June 17, 2026 deprecation of Gemini-2.5-Pro, we benchmarked three frontier reasoning models... GPT-5.5 wins on Overall, PAR, and FAS. We are adopting it as the new RACE evaluator (with GPT-5.4-mini for the FACT pipeline)." So data_gpt55 is the current tab, not the stale paper numbers. The local cached copy at /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/leaderboard_gpt55_judge.csv is byte-identical to the live file.

NUMBERS (all CONFIRMED). GPT-5.5 top-6 = cellcog-max, WhaleCloud-DocChain_0612, bodhi, lunon_full100_FINAL.submission, dalpha-deepresearch, sourcery.
- readability: 51.94 / 52.48 / 51.87 / 50.48 / 53.20 / 49.68 -> span 49.68-53.20, mean 51.61 ("~51.6" correct).
- insight: 57.08 / 55.33 / 54.60 / 54.83 / 52.94 / 52.22 -> span 52.22-57.08 correct.
- comprehensiveness: 56.34 / 55.14 / 54.15 / 53.42 / 52.58 / 50.53 -> span 50.53-56.34 correct.
- "nobody scores >53.2 readability": true across the ENTIRE GPT-5.5 tab, not just top-6 (max = dalpha 53.20).
- cellcog-max cross-judge: Gemini insight 60.01 / readability 53.21; GPT-5.5 insight 57.08 / readability 51.94. Exact.

"BARELY BEATS THE HUMAN REFERENCE" (CONFIRMED, mechanism verified). RACE normalizes each dimension as target/(target+reference), so a report that ties the reference scores exactly 50 (confirmed in deepresearch_bench_race.py). Best readability on the whole leaderboard is therefore +3.20 over the human reference, vs +7.08 on insight and +6.34 on comprehensiveness. Readability genuinely is the flattest dimension with the least headroom.

OUR DEFICIT (CONFIRMED, from our own official-harness run). Rank10 readability 0.3774 is recorded in /home/polaris/polaris_project/FLYWHEEL_PROGRESS.md:133 and /home/polaris/polaris_project/SOTA_REVIEW_BRIEF.md:32, scored with the official RACE harness under the gpt-5.5 evaluator. 51.61 - 37.74 = 13.87 ("~14 below the band") and 50 - 37.74 = 12.26 ("~12 below the reference"). It IS our largest per-dimension deficit: against the top-6 means the gaps are readability -13.87, insight -12.12 (42.38 vs 54.50), instruction-following -9.73, comprehensiveness -8.20. The 0.14 readability weight on task 72 is confirmed in /home/polaris/polaris_project/race_task72_criteria.txt:4.

ONE SUB-CLAIM IS WRONG (see `corrected`). "Under GPT-5.5 the judge is STINGIER on insight" is false as a general statement, and the claim's own caveat should be stronger: only 5 systems appear on BOTH tabs, and for 4 of them GPT-5.5 scores insight HIGHER than Gemini did (openai-deepresearch 43.73->46.69; perplexity 35.65->41.27; grok 30.89->38.12; gemini-2.5-pro-dr 49.45->49.92). Only cellcog-max — the single example the claim cites — goes down (60.01->57.08). The real effect is RANGE compression (GPT-5.5 pulls the insight scale in from both ends), not across-the-board stinginess. This does not change the operational conclusion (readability headroom is small, our readability deficit is the largest), but the causal story attached to it is over-generalized from one data point.

Two caveats worth carrying: (1) our 37.74 is task 72, n=1, while leaderboard numbers are 100-task means — the comparison is directional, not like-for-like; (2) the two tabs have almost entirely different system populations, so "same systems" is really only cellcog-max plus four low-ranked systems.
input.corrected: Everything in the claim reproduces exactly from the live current-judge source, EXCEPT the mechanism sentence "under GPT-5.5 the judge is stingier on insight." Corrected: GPT-5.5 COMPRESSES the insight range rather than lowering it. Of the only 5 systems present on both tabs, GPT-5.5 raises insight for 4 (openai-deepresearch 43.73->46.69, perplexity-Research 35.65->41.27, grok-deeper-search 30.89->38.12, gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch 49.45->49.92) and lowers it only for cellcog-max (60.01->57.08), the one case the claim cites. Sources: https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/raw/main/data_gpt55/leaderboard.csv and .../data/leaderboard.csv (Gemini tab; local copies at /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/leaderboard_gpt55_judge.csv and leaderboard_gemini25pro_judge.csv).
